schools.summary: God left David nearly a year until the child was born, breaking his heart by conscience before sending Nathan, who was already a trusted prophet.
Matthew Henry: David's harp was out of tune for nine months, yet God sent not an executioner but a friend, refusing to let his people lie still in sin.
Gill: David lay without true sense of sin while enemies blasphemed, until Nathan was sent to awaken him and draw out the repentance of Psalm 51.
When ordinary means failed God took an extraordinary course, and Nathan's parable was framed to surprise David into sentencing himself.

debates.question: Was David wholly impenitent during the silent months, or privately grieving?

sides.position: he lay hardened and concealing, tormented in conscience (Psalm 32)
one may willingly hope he lamented in secret before God

schools.summary: The parable is designed to rouse indignation without betraying its aim, touching the meanness of the act rather than the special crimes; compare 2 Samuel 14 and 1 Kings 20.
Gill: the many flocks and herds signify David's many wives and concubines, so that his abundance left him no excuse.
Wesley: the many flocks note David's many wives and concubines.
Gill: the one ewe lamb is Uriah's single wife, younger than he; Abarbinel's guess that Uriah was an older widower is noted only as a supposition.
Ellicott: every circumstance is exquisitely contrived to heighten pity for the oppressed and indignation against the oppressor.
Wesley glosses 'bought' as the buying of wives; Clarke reads the lamb as a household pet and calls that the one detail slightly at variance with nature.
Gill records two readings of the traveller: Satan the restless tempter, or (with the Jewish writers) the evil concupiscence that comes as a passing guest and ends as master of the house.

debates.question: Who or what is the 'traveller' that comes to the rich man?

sides.position: Satan, the restless tempter who visits the good only as a passing wayfarer
the evil imagination or lust in David, which comes as a traveller and grows to be master

schools.summary: Ellicott: the quick blaze of anger proves David's generous impulses and sense of justice were not extinguished by his sin (cf. the Nabal episode).
Clarke and Wesley judge the death-sentence excessive — the law imposed only fourfold restitution (Exod. 22:1); David was severe to others while indulgent to himself.
Gill: David pronounced sentence taking the parable for a real fact, exactly as Nathan intended.

Ellicott takes 'fourfold' exactly as the Law prescribes (Exod. 22:1; Luke 19:8), treating the versions' sevenfold or fortyfold as mere human indignation; Keil turns to the shock of 'Thou art the man.'
Gill reports Kimchi's view that the dual number means eightfold, the rich robbing the poor deserving more.

debates.question: How many lambs does the fourfold restitution require?

sides.position: four, in exact accordance with the Law (Exod. 22:1)
eight, the dual form doubling the four against a rich oppressor

schools.summary: Ellicott: the sudden 'Thou art the man' roused a conscience only stupefied by prosperity, proving David no essentially bad man; compare the prophetic rebukes of Saul and Ahab.
Gill (with Henry): Nathan recites God's lavish gifts — kingdom, deliverance, his master's house — so that such bounty aggravates the covetous sin.
Matthew Henry: where God has given much he gives more, so his liberality is the great aggravation of desiring forbidden fruit.
Clarke: 'What a terrible word!' — David is transfixed, and the fourfold penalty returns in the deaths of four of his sons; Wesley: Nathan now speaks as God's ambassador.

Keil and Ellicott read the recital of gifts as heightening David's ingratitude and the unreasonableness of the sin; 'thy master's wives' invokes the Oriental custom of inheriting a predecessor's harem, not literal cohabitation.
Gill: the 'master's house' means Saul's family, wealth, and kingdom, forfeited through Ishbosheth and given to David.
Clarke: the phrase may signify only that David held absolute power over all Saul possessed, the harem being the token of succession.

debates.question: In what sense were Saul's wives 'given into David's bosom'?

sides.position: a customary formula for succession to all royal rights, not literal possession (only one wife and concubine are named)

schools.summary: Ellicott: the stronger second verb means 'murdered' — murder in God's eyes though done by the Ammonites' sword; Keil finds the aggravation in that very indirection.
Gill: the despised commandment is the sixth and seventh precepts; the essence of the sin is contempt of the divine law and lawgiver.
Clarke: David is the murderer, the Ammonite sword only his instrument; Wesley: he despised even the particular promise of 2 Samuel 7 and affronted the ordinance of marriage.
Keil and Ellicott read 'for ever' as bounded to David's own lifetime — a succession of woes from Amnon (13:29) through Absalom (18:14) to Adonijah (1 Kings 2:24).
Gill extends the sword beyond David's death into the wars of his posterity with Israel and other nations.
Henry: mercy and the sword can stand together — the pardoned may still lie long under affliction without losing the covenant.

debates.question: How far does 'the sword shall never depart' reach?

sides.position: as long as David lives — a bounded term realized in his sons' deaths
on into the wars of David's posterity as well

schools.summary: Keil: a twofold punishment answers the twofold sin — public murder and violation of his wives — fulfilled by Absalom (16:21-22) under the law of just retribution.
Clarke and Wesley read the 'giving' as permissive providence: God's providence, once David's friend, is now made his enemy; the prophecy is left dark lest its fulfilment be hindered.
Henry: the secret sin is repaid by open shame — blood for blood, uncleanness for uncleanness, as face answers face in a glass.
Gill: David had concealed the adultery by killing Uriah and hastening the marriage; God now overturns that concealment before the sun.
Keil: these words removed the ban of hardening; David's very few words, like the publican's (Luke 18:13), are a mark of a thoroughly broken, unpalliating spirit.

Ellicott: David's 'I have sinned' is a broken heart poured out before God, unlike Saul's calculated concession; the pardon is chiefly an assurance of forgiveness, since no human court threatened the king.
Clarke and Wesley limit 'thou shalt not die' to temporal death; Clarke argues Psalm 51, if later, shows full pardon not yet received, and reads the Hebrew as sin 'transferred' to the child.
Gill (with Henry): the short confession was full and broken; 'thou shalt not die' means David shall not die eternally nor be put away from God.

debates.question: How much does 'thou shalt not die' grant?

sides.position: only a reprieve from temporal death, full pardon still to be sought
the settled forgiveness of the penitent, not to die eternally

schools.summary: Ellicott: the forgiven David must still suffer publicly to vindicate God's justice and deny the hope that sin goes unpunished; most of his griefs were the natural consequences that survive pardon.
Gill (with Henry): the complicated wickedness gave God's enemies occasion to blaspheme (Rom. 2:24), the peculiar evil of a professor's scandalous sin.
Gill: Nathan departed having done his errand, the pardon declared and the child's death foretold.

Henry: Nathan went home to pray, and was so honoured that David named a son Nathan in Christ's line; the child's sickness proves original sin, and David's fasting on the earth proves the reality of his repentance.

Ellicott (with von Gerlach): the prayer sprang not from want of submission nor undue love but from a hope to read God's restored favour in the child's preservation; Keil notes 'he came' means his chamber, not the sanctuary.
Wesley and Clarke: David prayed supposing the threat conditional, as all under the Law held God's threatenings to be, though Clarke owns the sentence could not be reversed.

debates.question: How could David pray after so plain a sentence of death?

sides.position: he held the threat conditional and preventable by prayer
he sought a token of restored divine favour, in full submission to God's will

schools.summary: Gill: the elders were the chief court officers come to raise David and get him to eat.
Wesley: David's excessive mourning sprang not from fear of losing the child but from a deep sense of his sin and the divine displeasure it signified.
Keil: the servants, having failed to move him while the child lived, feared he might do himself harm when told of its death.
Gill: the seventh day is counted from the child's birth, since David cannot be thought to have fasted a full week.
Wesley: the seventh day is reckoned from the beginning of the distemper.
Keil: the servants feared to tell David of the death lest, in his anguish, he do himself harm.

debates.question: From what point is the 'seventh day' counted?

sides.position: from the child's birth (David would not have fasted seven whole days)
from the beginning of the illness

schools.summary: Gill: the servants whispered low so David might not hear, but that very whispering told him the child was dead.
Keil: David saw at once from their conference what had happened, and on learning it rose, washed, worshipped in the house of the Lord, and ate.

themes: when to plead and when to submit

schools.summary: Keil: David laid aside all signs of penitential mourning and went first to worship in the holy tent on Zion; Clarke: washing and anointing were the East's first signs of leaving mourning.
Wesley: David came to the tabernacle to confess his sin, own God's justice, acknowledge mercy in sparing his life, and offer sacrifice.
Gill: he arose from the floor on which he had lain.
Gill: the servants knew what David had done but not its meaning, and that meaning was what they asked after.
Wesley (from the surrounding verses): David's grief was rooted in the sense of sin and God's displeasure, so it kept a season for pleading and a season for submission the servants could not read.
Clarke: all under the Mosaic dispensation held God's threatenings conditional, so David prayed on an unexpressed 'unless,' and ceased when grief became fruitless.
Keil (with von Gerlach, cited by Ellicott): David's 'Who can tell?' is humble hope, not presumption — a plea for a token of restored favour, not a want of submission.
Ellicott: David, being no Stoic, means 'I shall go to him' as confidence in a conscious life beyond the grave and a future recognition of those loved on earth.
Clarke: the clause may mean only 'I too shall die,' yet the reunion of friends in conscious existence is a solid consolation the very heathen reached after (Cicero's Cato).
Gill: further fasting is to no purpose, for the child cannot be brought back.

debates.question: Does 'I shall go to him' teach a conscious afterlife and reunion?

sides.position: yes — confidence in conscious existence beyond death and future recognition
perhaps only 'I also shall die,' though the reunion is nonetheless true

schools.summary: Keil: 'Solomon' means man of peace (1 Chron. 22:9), a pledge of David's restored peace with God; Ellicott: the birth, later than vv.26-31, is placed here to close Bathsheba's story and open the Messianic line.
Gill: now first called David's wife, Bathsheba is comforted with the assurance of pardon and of a son to succeed and build God's house.
Wesley: God declared he loved the son despite David's provocation; Clarke: 'Solomon' the peaceable, named for the peace of his reign.
Gill: most likely the Lord sent a message by Nathan that he loved Solomon, and so the name Jedidiah, 'beloved of the LORD,' was given.
Ellicott: Jedidiah only expressed the divine acceptance of Solomon and never became a personal title in use.
Clarke notes the rare case of a prophet naming a servant's child, yet the father's name prevailed; Wesley: 'beloved of the Lord,' for God's love or command.
Ellicott and Clarke locate Rabbah's city and citadel (cf. 1 Chron. 20:2), explaining why Joab, having taken the royal city, summoned David to finish the conquest.
Gill: the siege's completion is placed here, out of order, so the Bathsheba story may stand uninterrupted.
Henry: God graciously gave victory even before David repented, that his goodness might lead to repentance (Ps. 103:10); Joab faithfully sought his master's honour (Ps. 115:1).
Clarke reads the 'city of waters' as the reservoir-city, or better 'I have cut off the waters' (Josephus, Houbigant), making haste urgent; Wesley: it was encompassed with water.
Keil: David's need to gather all the people shows Joab's army was much weakened, so real force was wanted for the acropolis; Joab summons David to spare his own glory.
Gill: Joab sent to acquaint David with his progress and success.
Ellicott: Joab's call for a muster answered either a real need of force or the wish that David take the city formally in person.
Wesley: having taken one part, Joab was sure the rest could not hold, and summoned David lest he himself have the honour of taking it.
Gill: Joab bids David gather the rest of the soldiers in the land and come to Rabbah.

themes: lest it be called after my name

schools.summary: Gill: David gathered the people both to recruit Joab's diminished troops and to make further conquests and spoil of the Ammonite cities, then stormed and took Rabbah.

Ellicott: 'their king' may spell Milcom, but the idol-crown reading is needless; the talent may be a lighter Eastern weight. Keil: taken 'from his head' implies the king was captured or slain, and the talent-weight is an over-high estimate.
Clarke and Wesley read the talent as the crown's value, not weight, since jewels are not weighed by gold and the mass would be too great to wear.
Gill: it was the crown of Hanun the Ammonite king, stripped of his royalty by David.
Henry: David would set the costly crown on his head when he had better cast it at God's feet — the truly humbled heart is dead to worldly glory.

debates.question: Does 'a talent of gold' give the crown's weight or its value?

sides.position: its weight — an over-high estimate, or a lighter Eastern talent
its value or price, since the jewels are not weighed by gold and the mass would be unwearable

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott adopt the one-letter emendation ('he sawed them,' cf. 1 Chron. 20:3; Heb. 11:37), reading a real execution with iron tools and possibly Molech's fire, though Ellicott pleads David acted by the custom of his age.

Clarke argues the text means enslavement to sawing, mining, and brickmaking, not butchery, blaming a false translation; Wesley reads it as sawing them to death and passing them through Moloch's furnace, restrained to the guilty counsellors.
Gill limits the punishment to Hanun's princes who counselled the abuse of David's ambassadors.
Henry: however read, the severity ill became David and showed his heart not yet softened by repentance; we forgive best when we most feel our need of forgiveness.

debates.question: Did David torture the Ammonites to death, or reduce them to forced labour?

sides.position: execution by saws and iron tools (emending to 'he sawed them,' cf. 1 Chron. 20:3)
enslavement to sawing, mining, and brickmaking, not butchery of human beings
